⚠ Crew Escalation Process
▤ Section Flashcards

Quick-recall flashcards organized by training section, then module, then topic. Click a tile to drill in.

Deck Card 1 of 1
Term—Tap card to reveal
Definition—Tap to flip back
✈ Flight Planning — Step-by-Step Guide
Initial Check
1
Verify flight info in N-FP matches N-OC (correct tail / flight number / ETD).
2
Check status — is the flight going to be delayed further? Potential OOS or swap?
3
Identify flight type by flight number (scheduled / charter / repo / maintenance ferry / overnight delay).
4
Is this a 121 Scheduled/Supplemental or Part 91 Operation?
5
Special flight considerations — Landing Gear Down / Special Flight Permit Ferry.
Planning
1
Destination airport — Is weather at ETA above legal landing minimums / is it safe to operate on time? (Check if CPT is high mins.)
a) If legal and safe, is a destination alternate required?
b) If alternate not required, is there any reason to add a precautionary alternate?
c) Identify diversion risk. If moderate to high, consult DM or lead dispatcher.
d) Check ATC implications (active/planned GDP / GS / TFR / VIP events / EDCTs).
⚠ Check NOTAMs when going over these steps. Any airport restrictions or landing min impacts?
2
Departure airport — Is weather legal/safe for takeoff? Is a takeoff alternate required?
⚠ Check NOTAMs.
3
MEL/CDLs — Are there any items with performance restrictions or environmental limitations? Look up all MEL/CDLs in the manual.
4
Plan Route ("Route" tab)
a) Adverse weather (thunderstorms, turbulence, headwinds)
b) ATC impacts (active or planned reroutes)
c) Build/select route and cruise altitude (use FlightAware IFR Route Analyzer for route history)
d) Runway selection ("Use as Template" button) — Check ATIS, NOTAMs/forecast winds, runway length
5
Fuel considerations
a) Is ballast required?
b) Any reason to add hold, contingency, extra, or tanker fuel?
6
Mach speed — Is there any reason to speed up?
a) Delays — Block/FDP risk
b) Is it safe to do so? (Consider destination weather and timing. Prioritize fuel when necessary.)
7
Payload
a) Supplemental ops — identify if using actual weights
b) Overnight/significant delays — check NS2/Navitaire for accurate pax load
c) Add any COMAT as cargo
8
Check crew — any missing pilots or FAs? Dual CPT? Any JS? Verify crew discrepancies in N-OC and with Crew Services.
9
Add Release Remarks / ATC Remarks.
10
Run TLR.
a) Make sure correct runways are selected
b) Select runway contamination
c) Know when to plan for winds (improving or cautious with takeoff performance?)
d) Ensure MEL/CDL takeoff/landing performance penalties are applied
e) Is engine/wing/cowl anti-ice required ON?
f) Review takeoff/landing calculation results (Max restricted takeoff weight)
11
Compute and review flight plan. Is anything missing or does anything need to be changed? (Landing/Takeoff weights, ATC strip, tanker amount, fuel, cruise FL, alternate summary, MEL/CDLs, remarks)
12
RAIM Check — follow procedures listed in IOCM if not passed.
13
Review TLR — ensure MEL/CDL takeoff/landing performance penalties and runway contamination are applied. Any data missing?
14
Send Flight Package — ensure TLR is generated before sending.
15
File ATC Strip.
📄 Dispatch Release Explanation Guide

Click each page to view full size. Numbers on the images correspond to the reference table below.

Page 1 — Header & ATC Strip
Page 2 — Fuels, Weights & Route
Page 3 — Escape Routes & Alternates

🔍 Click any image to open full size in a new tab.

#FieldDescription
1Flight / Airline / TypeFlight Number, Airline Code (MXY), Statement of type of flight (IFR)
2Origin / Dest / Alt / Tkof Alt / ETD / ETAOrigin, Destination, 1st Destination Alternate (additional alternates in alternate summary box), Takeoff Alternate, ETD, ETA
3AircraftAircraft Type / Aircraft Registration Number
4Dispatcher / RAIMDispatcher Name, Time & Date of Computation / PASS = RAIM Check passed
5Revision / FP ID / Groups / PhoneRevision Number (increments by 1 per computation) / Unique Flight Plan ID / Desk assigned / IOCC Phone Number
6CrewList of all crew names
7High MinimumsHigh Minimums Captain designator
8RemarksRemarks section
9MEL / CDLApplied MELs and/or CDLs
10ATC Strip1. Filed IFR FPL, flight type (I=IFR, S=Scheduled)   2. Equipment type, wake turbulence category, equipment codes (S=VHF/VOR/ILS, D=DME, G=GPS, I=IRU, W=RVSM, E3=ACARS/PDC, R=PBN, E=Mode S/ACID/Alt, B2=1090MHz)   3. Departure / ETD   4. KTAS / Filed Alt / Route   5. Destination / ETE / Alternate. Plus PBN section, DOF, REG, SELCAL.
11Route SummaryFiled Route Summary
12CI / FLCost Index / Filed Flight Level
13Avg Wind / ISAAverage wind and ISA deviations used in planning
14Fuel Bias / PFACTFuel bias and/or factor to account for increased burn
15MIN TO (MINTO)FAR 121.639 minimum fuel for takeoff — includes enroute burn, alternate (most distant), holding, contingency, ballast, and reserve. Also called "Above-the-line Fuel"
16Extra FuelAdditional fuel not designated as required fuel
17RAMP / REMFPlanned Ramp (block out) fuel weight & time endurance / Planned remaining arrival fuel weight & time endurance
18ZFW BurnAdditional trip fuel burn associated with 1,000 lb increase in ZFW
19Payload / MFLPlanned Payload / Maximum planned flight level
20DISTStraight-line distance (DIST) / Total distance in nautical air miles
21BOWBasic Operating Weight
22Payload / ZFWPlanned Payload / Planned Zero Fuel Weight
23TOFPlanned takeoff fuel weight and time endurance
24TOGW / Burn / LDGWPlanned Takeoff Weight / Planned trip burn / Planned Landing Weight
25MZFWMaximum zero fuel weight
26MTOWMaximum takeoff weight (lower of structural or operational)
27MLDWMaximum landing weight (lower of structural or operational)
28WPT / FRQWaypoint name or identifier / Frequency change
29LAT/LONG / RTE / MORALatitude & Longitude / Route Identifier / MORA for this flight segment
30MC / MHMagnetic Course / Magnetic Heading
31DIST / TCDistance of this flight leg / True Course
32TAS / GSTrue Air Speed / Ground Speed
33DV / WSISA Deviation / Wind Shear (higher number = rougher air)
34LEG-T / TOT-TLeg Time / Total Time
35ETA / ATAEstimated Time of Arrival / Actual Time of Arrival
36FLFlight Level
37TEMP / WIND/COMPTemperature / Wind direction and speed / Wind component
38PFRM / AFRMPlanned Fuel Remaining / Actual Fuel Remaining
39Escape Routes — TIMETime in mins to alternate via escape routing
40Escape RoutingRouting towards escape alternate
41Destination AlternateDestination Alternate Airport
42–48Alternate SummaryGMA / Mileage / True track / Wind component / FL / Trip time / Fuel burn to alternate
52–53Wind SummaryWind summary — Waypoint name / Flight level of report
54SignatureFit for duty / Dispatch Release signature (Captain + FO)
🔎 Thorough Release Review Guide
Flight Plan Section
▪
Flight Number — What type of flight is it? (Domestic, Live Charter, Charter Repo, Repo, International Supplemental)
If release has expired, update times and weather. Release expires 2 hours after ETD (IOCM 4.2.1.2).
▪
Origin / Destination / Alternate / Takeoff Alternate / ETD / ETA
▪
Equipment / Registration — Last minute swaps can happen. Verify aircraft on release matches N-OC. Contact DM for discrepancies.
▪
Dispatcher / Time / Date — Make sure your name is on the release. During training, Trainer's name must be on the release.
During OJT, trainees shall NOT save their trainer's password when logging into N-FP.
▪
RAIM Validation — Must say "PASS" or perform RAIM Validation process (IOCMTR-14: 4.4.9.5). If "RAIM NOT CHECKED" appears, click "RAIM Validate" then "Flight Plan" in Package Contents.
▪
Revision Number / Flight Plan ID — Revision number increments each time you compute. Verify which revision has been sent.
Crew Section
▪
Crew is present and matches N-OC.
▪
Dual Captain — If two Captains listed, N-OC should indicate which is LR (Lower Ranked). If N-OC does not show LR, refer to Crew Scheduling. Lower ranked listed as FO.
▪
High Minimums Captain (IOCM 4.4.3.3) — HM Captains get +100 ft and +½ SM to published landing minimums. Cannot be applied to Exemption 22897 (when approved). RVR minimum per IOCM 18.6.3 Table 18-1 and Breeze OpSpec C054.
MEL / CDL Section
▪
Dispatcher is responsible to ensure MEL/CDL limitations are not contradictory to any 14 CFR, Company policies, NOTAMs, TFRs, or aircraft operating procedures. (IOCM 21.3.2)
▪
Review MEL/CDL items and their specific conditions, provisions, and limitations: Operational limitations, Performance limitations, Performance restrictions (altitude, speed, weather), Operations procedures, W&B restrictions. (IOCM 21.4)
▪
N-FP "List" page — Restrictive MELs show in red. Non-restrictive show in black.
Fuels and Weights
▪
Standard landing fuel: 70 min / Minimum landing fuel: 60 min (IOCM 9.4)
If no alternate planned, system adds 15 min in ALT/NIL line to reach 60 min. If no additional fuel added, remaining 10 min (to reach 70-min standard) goes to XTRA LDG line.
▪
TOGW/MTOW and LDGW/MLDW — Must meet the company required 2,000 lb buffer between planned and Max weights.
▪
Payload — Live charter flights require payload to be manually input (found in Trip Sheet in Charter Trip Packages SharePoint). Diversion flights: payload does NOT transfer — must be added manually.
▪
PFACT — Shows if required by CDL or items requiring additional fuel burn calculation (e.g., WiFi radome on A220s). If multiple items make up PFACT, individual performance penalties are not visible.
Escape Routes
▪
MORA — Minimum Off-Route Altitude: provides minimum obstacle clearance above all obstacles (2,000 ft above mountainous/high terrain).
▪
Offsets — Distance from beginning of the leg (nm) at which the described escape route will be safe to use.
▪
Legal escape route alternates — Runway length ≥ 6,000 ft, meets alternate weather minimums, not required to be on C070 (emergency use only).
▪
Allow the computer to select escape route alternate stations first. If error, manually select. Allow system to select altitude. MELs with FL restrictions may limit selection — select a range the aircraft is capable of rather than locking a specific altitude.
▪
Enable terrain and escape routes overlay to see problem areas. Route adjustments away from higher terrain can assist. Adding/removing points near higher terrain can be a last-resort solution.
▪
Verify remark has been added: crews must contact Dispatch before accepting ATC reroutes.
Weather & NOTAMs
▪
METAR — Verify ALL 5 elements present: Winds, Visibility, Sky condition, Temperature, Altimeter. RVR is controlling for takeoffs/landings and straight-in instrument approaches for that specific runway (IOCM 18.5.1.3). METAR valid: 1 hour.
▪
TAF — Verify no missing elements and that it is present (IOCM 18.4.1). TAF valid: 24–30 hours, issued at 0000, 0600, 1200, 1800Z. RAMTAF request required if elements within or entire TAF is missing.
▪
Verify weather is present for ALL stations on the release: origin, destination, takeoff alternate, escape route alternates, and driftdown alternates.
⚠ WSI TAFs will NOT show in the Weather & NOTAMs package.
▪
NOTAMs — Briefly review to catch any new NOTAMs added since initial scan. New NOTAMs towards the top, split by category (ILS, APRON, TWY, RWY, etc.). Includes all stations on release.
▪
Company NOTAMs (IOCM 24.7) — New Company NOTAMs on bottom. Can be added/removed at any time.
Runway Analysis (RWA)
▪
Verify RWA has returned prior to sending. Scan entire RWA — missing data shows as dashes.
▪
Takeoff data: Airport, Planned Runway, OAT, Max Runway Takeoff Weight, V speeds, Planned TOW vs Max Flight Plan TOW (also shows 2,000 lb buffer).
▪
Landing data: Airport, Planned Runway, OAT, Max Runway Landing Weight, Planned Landing Weight. "/" indicates what the system ran. Additional data gives numbers if they fall outside what the system ran — crews can depart as long as a TLR has returned with their data.
📝 Required Remarks Reference

Standard required remarks for common situations. Tailor each to the specific flight — check spelling and ensure remarks apply to the flight you are currently planning, especially when reusing remarks for similar city pairings.

Additional Fuel — Hold
HOLD FUEL ADDED FOR POSSIBLE DELAYS WITH CONVECTIVE WEATHER MOVING IN FROM THE SOUTHWEST WITH TOPS TO 350.
Additional Fuel — Contingency
CONTINGENCY FUEL ADDED FOR PIREPS AND TURBULENCE FORECAST INDICATING ROUGH RIDES FROM FL200-350. ADDITIONAL FUEL ADDED FOR POSSIBLE FLIGHT LEVEL ADJUSTMENTS.
Additional Fuel — Extra
EXTRA FUEL ADDED FOR LONG DEICE LINES DEPARTING DEN.
Precautionary Alternate
PRECAUTIONARY ALTERNATE ADDED — [REASON: e.g., DOWNWARD TRENDING VIS/CIGS / SMALL TEMP/DEWPOINT SPREAD WITH EXPECTED FOG UNTIL 1400Z PER FORECAST DISCUSSION / CONVECTIVE WEATHER EXPECTING TO BUILD JUST SOUTH OF AIRPORT WITH POTENTIAL WINDSHEAR]
Part 91 Flight
CHARTER FERRY LEG PART 91 OPERATION
Tech on Board
TECH ON BOARD: [NAME] – [EMPLOYEE ID] THIS FLIGHT IS OPERATED UNDER 14 CFR PART 91. PLEASE ENSURE THAT GALLEY CARTS ARE ON BOARD AND STOWED PRIOR TO DEPARTURE.
RAMTAF
RAMTAF REQUESTED DUE TO ________. [PASTE FULL RAMTAF TEXT HERE]
⚠ OGS and HVN require WSI TAFs to be added manually.
High Minimums Captain
CAPTAIN RESTRICTED TO HIGH MINIMUMS UNDER 14 CFR 121.625
Low Time First Officer
LOW TIME FIRST OFFICER RESTRICTIONS APPLY UNDER 14 CFR 121.438
Increased Speed
INCREASED SPEED DUE TO HIGH BLOCK RISK.
Ballast Fuel
5000 LBS BALLAST FUEL HAS BEEN PLANNED AND IS CONSIDERED UNUSABLE
Reference: IOCM 9.7
Tanker Fuel
TANKERED FUEL FOR ECONOMICAL PURPOSES. USE AS NEEDED FOR DEVIATIONS.
Reference: Tanker guide on Dispatch Teams SharePoint
Performance Restrictive MEL — FL Restriction
FLIGHT LEVEL RSTR TO FL310 DUE TO MEL [ITEM NUMBER]
Performance Restrictive MEL — Penalties Applied
PERFORMANCE RESTRICTIONS APPLIED DUE TO MEL/CDL [ITEM NUMBER]
Reference: IOCM 4.4.7.3 NOTE
ACARS Unavailable
ACARS COMMUNICATIONS UNAVAILABLE. MONITOR ARINC VHF VOICE FREQUENCIES. COMMUNICATE OOOI TIMES WITH STATION OPERATIONS
RAIM Check Failure
RAIM CHECK FAIL OVER [LOCATION]. FILED ROUTE DOES NOT DEPEND ON RNAV IN AFFECTED AREA.
O2 Escape Routes
IF ALTERNATE AIRPORTS DESIGNATED FOR O2 ESCAPE SITUATIONS ARE MENTIONED IN THE ALTERNATE SUMMARY, PLEASE REFRAIN FROM ACCEPTING ATC REROUTES UNTIL YOU HAVE CONTACTED DISPATCH FOR GUIDANCE
Step Climb
STEP CLIMB PLANNED AT [WAYPOINT] FROM FL360 TO FL380
Within 2,000 lb Buffer — MTOW/MLDW
PLANNED WITHIN 2000LB BUFFER DUE TO ADDITIONAL FUEL REQUIREMENTS FOR [REASON]. PLANNED WITHIN 2000LB BUFFER DUE TO [REASON]
Revision Update
REV [#] COMPUTED DUE TO [REASON]
SAN Curfew
SAN - ARRIVALS ARE ALLOWED 24 HOURS A DAY BUT WE CAN ONLY DEPART BETWEEN 0700L AND 2330L DUE TO CURFEW.
SNA Curfew
SNA NOISE CURFEW HOURS MUST BE OBSERVED BY DISPATCH AND PIC. FLIGHTS SHALL ONLY BE ALLOWED TO ARRIVE BETWEEN THE HOURS OF 0700-2300L MON-SAT AND 0800-2300L SUN. FLIGHTS SHALL ONLY BE ALLOWED TO DEPART BETWEEN THE HOURS OF 0700-2200L MON-SAT AND 0800-2200L SUN. DUE TO NOISE SENSORS, AIRCRAFT MUST BE WHEELS UP 10 MINUTES PRIOR (2150L) TO DEPARTURE CURFEWS, AND CANNOT PASS DSNEE 5 MINUTES (0655L MON-SAT OR 0755L SUN) OR KLEVR 10 MINUTES (0650L MON-SAT OR 0750L SUN) PRIOR TO ARRIVAL TO CURFEW LIFTING PLEASE CLOSE DOOR AS CLOSE TO D-15 AS POSSIBLE DUE TO ENGINE START PROCEDURES DELAYING WHEEL MOVEMENT FOR OUT TIMES.
✅ Release Review Checklist

Check each item before sending the release package. Click to mark complete.

🌬 Wind Summary Decode Guide

The Wind Summary uses encoded wind data. Here is how to decode each group.

Rule 1 — Speed > 100 knots (subtract 50 from direction, add 100 to speed)
731960
Step 1: Direction — 73 − 50 = 23 → 230°
Step 2: Speed — 19 + 100 = 119 knots
Step 3: Temp — −60°C
Result: 230° @ 119 kts, −60°C
Rule 2 — Speed 200+ knots
189960
Coded as 99 kts → actual speed is 200+ knots
Result: 180° @ 200+ kts, −60°C
Rule 3 — Light and Variable (< 5 kts)
9900
Coded group 9900 = winds are light and variable
Special Cases
▪
No winds are forecast within 1,500 feet of station elevation.
▪
No temperatures are forecasted within 2,500 feet AGL of station elevation.
▪
Temperatures above 24,000 feet are negative (do not need a minus sign in the decode).
Wind Shear (WS) Column in Route Details
!
The higher the Wind Shear (WS) number, the rougher the air. This is a good indicator for altitude selection to keep flights out of turbulence.
⚖ Exemption 22897

Reference: IOCMTR-20  ·  Recurrent Training October 2024

What is Exemption 22897?
Exemption 22897 allows the dispatcher to release a flight to an airport or alternate when the TAF indicates, through conditional statements (TEMPO, PROB, BECMG) that the weather could be below authorized landing minimums at the estimated time of arrival.

The main body of the forecast MUST indicate that the weather will be AT or ABOVE authorized minimums at the estimated time of arrival.

Reference: IOCM Bulletins  ·  IOCMTR-20 EXEMPTION 22897

Marginal Weather — IOCM 8.2.5
▪
Destination is marginal when ceiling is forecast below 1,000 ft AGL and visibility is below 3 miles.
▪
First alternate is marginal when ceiling is forecast below 600 ft AGL and visibility is below 2 miles.
▪
When both the destination and first alternate weather conditions are marginal, a second alternate is required.
Application of 22897
1
Add a second destination alternate whenever the forecast for the destination and/or first alternate indicates — using conditional language (TEMPO or PROB) — that weather could be less than authorized minimums.
2
Dispatcher must remark on the release:
EXEMPTION 22897 APPLIED. PLEASE REVIEW THE ALTERNATES IN ACCORDANCE WITH A005 AND 22897.
Conditional Language Requirements
AirportMain Body RequirementConditional Language Minimum
Destination AT or ABOVE authorized landing minimums Visibility no less than ½ of the lowest minimum visibility for the approach expected to be used
First Alternate AT or ABOVE alternate minimums (ceiling and visibility) No less than ½ of the alternate minimum ceiling and visibility
Second Alternate AT or ABOVE alternate minimums Conditional language must be at or above alternate minimums — no reduction allowed
TAF Examples
Destination — KTPA   ETA 1225Z
TAF
KTPA 140520Z1406/1506 16004KT 4SM BR BKN025 FM141100 17003KT 2SM BR SCT009 BKN015 TEMPO 1411/1414 1/4SM FG BKN001 FM141600 18012KT 3SM SCT025 BKN040 FM142300 18014G20KT P6SM -TSRA BKN030CB FM150100 33015G25KT P6SM SCT090
✓
Main body at ETA (1225Z): FM141100 shows 2SM visibility — AT or ABOVE landing minimums for visibility.
⚠
TEMPO 1411/1414: 1/4SM — verify this is no less than ½ of the lowest minimum visibility for the expected approach.
First Alternate — KMCO   Alternate Mins: 400 ft / 1 SM   ETA 1321Z at destination
TAF
KMCO 140520Z1406/1506 16004KT 6SM BR BKN025 FM141100 17003KT 4SM BR SCT009 BKN015 TEMPO 1411/1414 1/2SM FG BKN002 FM141600 18012KT 3SM SCT010 BKN040 FM142300 18008G15KT P6SM -TSRA BKN050
✓
Main body: AT or ABOVE alternate minimums (400 ft / 1 SM) for ceiling and visibility.
⚠
TEMPO ceiling: BKN002 = 200 ft — verify ≥ ½ of 400 ft (200 ft) ✓   Visibility: 1/2 SM — verify ≥ ½ of 1 SM (½ SM) ✓
Second Alternate — KRIC   Alternate Mins: 500 ft / 1¼ SM   ETA 1129Z at destination
TAF
KRIC 140520Z1406/1506 16004KT 4SM BR BKN025 FM141100 02010KT 1 1/2SM BR SCT009 BKN015 TEMPO 1411/1414 1SM FG BKN005 FM141600 18012KT 4SM SCT025 BKN040 FM142300 18014G20KT P6SM -TSRA BKN030CB
✓
Main body: AT or ABOVE alternate minimums.
✓
TEMPO: BKN005 = 500 ft ≥ 500 ft alternate min ✓   1 SM < 1¼ SM — FAILS. Second alternate conditional language must be AT or ABOVE alternate minimums — no reduction. This TAF would NOT qualify as a legal second alternate under 22897.
RNAV / GPS Considerations
▪
The company is authorized to use GPS-based IAPs at either the destination or alternate airport — not both. Planning for GPS-based IAP must be based on a single NAVAID, even if there are two or more RNAV (GPS) approaches to different adequate runways, when using derived alternate weather minimums.
Exemption Does NOT Apply To
✗
Destinations or alternate cities outside the 48 contiguous United States
✗
High Minimums Captains
✗
The ceiling requirement of a circle-to-land approach
✗
Part 121 Supplemental operations
✗
Part 91 operations
✈ FAA ICAO Flight Plan Quick Reference

Version 8  ·  September 2022  ·  Reference: AIM Appendix 4 / AIP Appendix 2

Page 1 — FPL Format, PBN Codes, Equipment
Page 2 — Items 10a, 10b & Item 18 Fields

🔍 Click any image to open full size in a new tab.

FPL Format
ICAO FPL Structure
(FPL-ACID-Flt Rules/Flight Type -AC Type/Wake Cat/Equip & Capability -Departure/EOBT -Speed/Altitude [sp] Route -Destination/ETE [sp] Alternate(s) -Other Information)
Example
(FPL-TTT123-IS -C550/L-SDE1E2GHIJ3J5RWZ/SB1D1 -KPWM1225 -N0440F310 SSOXS5 SSOXS DCT BUZRD DCT SEY DCT HTO J174 ORF J121 CHS EESNT LUNNI1 -KJAX0214 KMCO -PBN/A1L1B1C1D1O1T1 NAV/Z1 GBAS DAT/1FANS2PDC SUR/260B RSP180 DOF/220501 REG/N123A SEL/BPAM CODE/A05ED7)
Item 10a — Navigation, Communications & Approach Aid
CodeMeaning
NNo capabilities
SStandard (VOR, VHF, ILS)
ZOther capabilities — indicate in NAV/, DAT/, or COM/. Always include Z when filing NAV/
WRVSM — file only if authorized for RVSM operations
GGNSS
IINS
DDME
RPBN — include type in Item 18 PBN/
LILS
E1ACARS FMC WPR
E2ACARS D-FIS
E3ACARS PDC
J1CPDLC ATN VDL Mode 2
J4CPDLC FANS 1/A VDL Mode 2
J5CPDLC FANS 1/A Satellite Inmarsat
J7CPDLC FANS 1/A Satellite Iridium
Item 10b — Surveillance
CodeMeaning
NNo capabilities
CTransponder Modes A and C
SMode S — ACID and Altitude
EMode S — ACID, Altitude, Extended Squitter
LMode S — ACID, Altitude, Enhanced Surveillance, Extended Squitter
B1ADS-B 1090 MHz "out"
B2ADS-B 1090 MHz "out" and "in"
U1ADS-B UAT "out"
U2ADS-B UAT "out" and "in"
D1ADS-C FANS 1/A
G1ADS-C ATN
Item 18 — Other Information Fields
FieldDescriptionExample
STS/Special handling reasonSTS/MEDEVAC
PBN/Performance Based Navigation capabilities (up to 8)PBN/D1O2
NAV/Other navigation capability — requires Z in Item 10aNAV/Z1
COM/Other communications capability
DAT/Other data application (FANS, PDC)DAT/1FANS2PDC
SUR/Other surveillance capabilitySUR/260B RSP180
DOF/Date of flight (YYMMDD)DOF/220501
REG/Aircraft registrationREG/N123A
SEL/SELCAL codeSEL/BPAM
CODE/Aircraft Mode S address (hex)CODE/A05ED7
RMK/Remarks — any information instructed to includeRMK/DVRSN
EET/Estimated elapsed timesEET/KZNY0124
TALT/Takeoff alternateTALT/KTEB
RALT/Enroute alternatesRALT/EINN CYYR
PER/Performance categoryPER/C
OPR/Operator when not evident from ACID
Item 18 PBN/ Codes
CodeCapability
A1RNAV 10 (RNP 10)
L1RNP 4
B1/B2/B3/B4/B5RNAV 5 — All / GNSS / DME/DME / VOR/DME / INS or IRS
C1/C2/C4RNAV 2 — All / GNSS / DME/DME/IRU
D1/D2/D4RNAV 1 — All / GNSS / DME/DME/IRU
O1/O2RNP 1 — All / GNSS
S1/S2RNP Approach / RNP APCH with Baro VNAV
T1RNP AR Approach with RF
Z1/Z2Additional: Radius to Fix (RF) / Fixed Radius Transitions (FRT)
P1Advanced RNP (A-RNP)
M1/M2RNP 2 Continental / Oceanic
STS/ Special Handling Reasons
▪
ALTRV — Altitude reservation
▪
ATFMX — Exempt from ATFM
▪
FFR — Fire fighting
▪
FLTCK — Flight check
▪
HAZMAT — Hazardous materials
▪
HEAD — Head of state
▪
HUM — Humanitarian
▪
HOSP — Medical flight
▪
MEDEVAC — Life-critical medical flight
▪
SAR — Search and rescue
▪
NONRVSM — Non-RVSM requesting operations in RVSM airspace (ensure W is NOT filed in Item 10a)
Performance Categories (PER/)
CategorySpeed (Vref or 1.3Vso at max landing weight)
ALess than 91 knots IAS
B91 to less than 121 knots IAS
C121 to less than 141 knots IAS
D141 to less than 166 knots IAS
E166 to less than 211 knots IAS
HHelicopters
🌎 International Supplemental Operations

Reference: SPATCH Learning Lab  ·  IOCM Chapter 27  ·  § 121.645

Overview — Topics Covered
▪
Flight Numbers
▪
Documents & Permits
▪
Meteorology & NOTAMs
▪
Fuel & Alternate Requirements
▪
Diversions & Emergencies
Flight Numbers
▪
Live International Operations use the 6100–6199 series flight numbers.
Jeppesen E-Link
▪
Breeze uses Jeppesen to consolidate international operational information. Jeppesen manuals are found online in Jeppesen E-Link for dispatcher use.
Includes approach plates, enroute charts, country entry requirements, regulatory differences, emergencies, radio aids, and more.
▪
Refer to IOCM 27.4.1 for procedures for accessing the Jeppesen Application. Also refer to IOCM Ch 27.
Documents — General Declaration (GenDec)
▪
The General Declaration / Crew Manifest (GenDec) must be carried on board all Breeze international flights.
▪
Provided by the departure station GSA to the OCR (Onboard Charter Representative) for charter operations.
▪
It is ultimately the PIC's responsibility to ensure accuracy and ensure this form is on board (unless maintained electronically).
▪
GenDec includes:
N number (tail #)  ·  Flight number  ·  Number of crewmembers  ·  Number of passengers
▪
Located in the Charter Trip Package. Remains in the International Documents bag until surrendered at the destination.
Overflight & Landing Permits
▪
For certain international operations, landing or overflight permits may be required. Each flight is evaluated by the Director of Charter Operations, Director of Charter Sales, or their designee.
▪
Each trip sheet will indicate whether a permit is required. A copy must be attached to the charter trip sheet on the company intranet for dispatcher review prior to transmitting the release.
▪
Dispatchers remark the permit number of any overflight permit in the FPM within the ICAO ATC strip RMK/18 section.
Meteorology & NOTAMs
▪
BECMG — likely only encountered when operating in Canada.
• Gradual change in conditions expected over a period of no more than 2 hours.
• Only changing conditions are included — omitted conditions carry over from the previous group.
• Once time falls outside the BECMG timeframe, it becomes the main body line.
▪
International NOTAMs — captured by the FPM and presented in the same format as domestic NOTAMs. ICAO format NOTAMs can also be found at notams.aim.faa.gov.
Fuel Supply — § 121.645
For flag or supplemental operations outside the 48 contiguous United States, the aircraft must have enough fuel to:

1. Fly to and land at the destination airport
2. After that, fly for 10% of the total time from departure to destination
3. After that, fly to and land at the most distant alternate
4. After that, fly for 30 minutes holding at 1,500 ft above the alternate (or destination if no alternate required)
⚠
Breeze policy is more restrictive — required to have 45 minutes of reserve fuel OR 10% of total enroute, whichever is greater. Reserve fuel requirements may exceed the FAR amounts based on the type of operation.
▪
Reserve Policy in N-FP — Flag & International Supplemental should default to Reserve Policy 90. Verify this is set correctly.
▪
Inflight Fuel Calculations — switch Reserve Fuel Policy back to "1" (45 minutes) to ensure accurate minimum/emergency fuel calculations during diversions or reroutes.
Alternates
▪
Weather requirements at the alternate airport must meet the requirements of the certificate holder's operations specifications. Refer to OpSpec C055.
Diversions
▪
Best choice for a diversion in the U.S. or Mexico is to an airport that can provide customs clearance.
▪
Departing from the U.S. — alternate selection within the U.S. is preferred when possible.
▪
Departing to the U.S. — alternate selection must have customs available.
▪
Diversion to an airport not listed in C070 is not recommended and should only be considered during an emergency.
▪
If diverting to an alternate outside the 48 contiguous U.S., refer to the IODG (International Operations Destination Guide) for preferred alternates — includes a list of all pre-cleared airports.
!
These are guidelines to help the operation run smoothly. Safety always takes priority!
